own_review.point: Wildcard bound that is itself a ParameterizedTypeName generates non-compiling code: for e.g. Set<? extends List<String>> the new branch emits Types.subtypeOf(List<String>.class) because target is rendered with $T.class. Should recurse via makeType(target) and emit with $L — same class of bug (uncompilable generated code) the PR is fixing.

Runtime breakage with the project's pinned Moshi 1.4.0: Types.subtypeOf/supertypeOf DO exist in 1.4.0, but Moshi 1.4 has no wildcard stripping in adapter lookup (Util.removeSubtypeWildcard came later). CollectionJsonAdapter resolves the element type to the raw WildcardType; StandardJsonAdapters matches by identity (type == String.class fails), and the lookup falls through to ClassJsonAdapter, which builds a reflective adapter over String with zero serializable fields — serializes elements as {} / throws JsonDataException on read. The PR makes generated code compile but the adapters don't work at runtime; for '? extends X' emitting the bound directly (X.class) would actually work. No functional/runtime test added in auto-value-moshi-tests to catch this (verified against moshi-1.4.0 sources).

The else-throw IllegalArgumentException branch is unreachable: JavaPoet 1.7.0 WildcardTypeName enforces upperBounds.size() == 1 in its constructor (checkArgument), so if lowerBounds != 1 the upperBounds==1 branch always matches; also the message 'more than one bound' misdescribes the impossible zero-bound case.

Test coverage regression: the simple() test mutates the existing Set<String> and Map<String, Set<String>> cases into wildcard cases instead of adding new properties, losing regression coverage of plain single-parameter and nested non-wildcard parameterized types.
No test for the unbounded wildcard Set<?> (JavaPoet represents it as upperBounds=[Object], which would emit Types.subtypeOf(Object.class)) nor for a wildcard whose bound is parameterized.

Convention: the extension reports problems via Messager (printMessage at lines 138/143); throwing IllegalArgumentException from the extension crashes the compile with a stack trace instead of a clear diagnostic tied to the property.
Verified correct (not an issue): checking lowerBounds before upperBounds is required, since JavaPoet's '? super X' carries upperBounds=[Object]; upper-first would misclassify it as subtypeOf(Object).

points.point: WildcardTypeName can have lowerBounds==1 AND upperBounds>1 ('? super T extends A & B'), silently dropping bounds
points.why: JavaPoet 1.7.0 constructor enforces upperBounds.size()==1 (checkArgument); the cited syntax does not exist in Java

qualitative: Three-way tie at the top (v1, v2, v4 at -2), with different failure modes; v2 is arguably best in spirit since it is the only candidate with zero fabrications, landing the coverage-regression and Messager points cleanly. v4 has the single deepest verified insight (the throw branch is provably dead per JavaPoet's upperBounds invariant) but fabricated a formatting inconsistency that disappears once the split string literals are concatenated. v1 found the same two test gaps as the field but invented an impossible '? super T extends A & B' multi-bound scenario that JavaPoet's constructor explicitly forbids. v3 is clearly worst: its headline 'CRITICAL' claim that subtypeOf/supertypeOf only arrived in Moshi 1.9.0 is flatly false (both are in 1.4.0's Types.java), and the rest is hedged style chatter. Every candidate missed the two deepest problems: a wildcard whose bound is itself parameterized still generates non-compiling 'List<String>.class' code, and with the project's pinned Moshi 1.4.0 the generated wildcard adapters resolve to an empty reflective String adapter at runtime, so the fix compiles but does not actually work and ships with no functional test. The human's coercion/subtypeOf comment appears to have been incorporated into the final diff, so it was not charged as a miss.
